air Political Practices CommissioIL 
80X 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
February 1, 1978 
Mr. John F. Henning 
'Executive Secretary-Treasurer 
California Labor Federation, AFL-CIO 
995 Market Street 
San Francisco, CA 
94103 
Re: 
Opinion Request No. 75-
6 
Dear Mr. Henning: 
On September 16, 1975, you requested an opinion from 
the Fair Political Practices Commission concerning whether cer-
tain proposed amendments to the California Labor Federation's 
Constitution altering the process by which the Federation would 
make political contributions would satisfy the req~irements of 
the Political Reform Act. 
Th'~ proposed amendments vlere as fol-
lows. 
Article XV I Section F would be amended by adding a ne'.'/ 
Section 1, which would provide: 
Officers of the Federation, members of the Executive 
Council and delegates to a Special Pre-Primary Elec-
tion Convention ~nd/or a Pre-General Election Con-
vention who are "lobbyists" within the meaning of 
the State of California Political Reform Act of 1974 
shall have voice but no vote in the political recom-
mendation and endorsement process relating to candi-
dates for State and Local offices outlined in this 
Article. 
Article XIX would be amended QY making Section 2 Section 3 
by adding a new Section 2, which would provide: 
The Standing Committee on Political Education shall 
proceed as follows in the making of political con-
tributions to candidates for State and Local office 
(who have 
ender 
Fursua~t to Art 
Ie XV F of 
s Constitution) wlthin the mean 
the Pcliti-
cal Reform Act: 


Mr. John F. Henning 
P. 2 
February 1, 1978 
(a) 
The Committee shall determine by a vote of those 
of its members who are not "lobbyistsll \vithin the 
meaning of the Political Reform Act t~e maximum amount 
of its Fund that may be used for the making of contri-
butions to such candidates, within the meaning of the 
Act, and the maximum amount that may be contributed 
to any such candidate or class of such candidates, 
in total and/or at anyone time. 
The Committee's 
Director shall provide such advice and information 
as the Co~~ittee requests but shall not make any 
recommendations as to the amounts to 
contributed. 
(b) 
The Committee shall by a vote of those of 
s 
members who are not "lobbyists" with 
the meaning 
of the Political Reform Act select from among its 
members who are not "lobbyists" as just defined, a 
three-member Subcommittee on State and ~ocal Office 
Political Contributions, and three alternates, and 
shall designate one of that number as the Subcommit-
tee's Chairman. 
The Committee's Director shall be 
a member of said Subcommittee ex-officio with vo 
but no vote. 
The Subcommittee shall have the au-
thority to receive monies utilized for the making of, 
and to make political contributions within the limits 
adopted pursuant to Section 2 of this Article by the 
full Committee. 
The Committee's Director shall pro-
vide such adv 
and informati.on as the Subcommittee 
requests but shall not make any recommendations as to 
the amounts to be contributed. 
On October 27, 1975, the Commission responded, in a let-
ter signed by Chairman Lowenstein, that the proposed amendments 
would not satisfy the requirements of the Act. 
Essential 
f the 
ssion concluded that the proposed amendments would permit 
the Federation's lobbyists, and in particular its Secretary-
Treasurer (who serves as the Director of the Federation's Commit-
tee on Political Education), to make "recom.rnendations" coz:cern 
political contributions and that, in light of the Commission's 
Green opinion, 1 FPPC Opinions 86 {No. 75-098, July 3, 1975}, 
such re~ommendations could constitute "arranging" for a contribu-
tion within the meaning of Gov. Code Section 36202. 
The Commis-
sion also observed that the proposed amendments were not respons 
to its earlier decision in the opinion requested by the California 
Labor Federation, 1 FPPC Opinions 28 (No. 75-004, June 18, 1975), 
that a lobbyist should not serve as the Director of the Committee 
on Political Education. 
On December 3, 1975, the Commission sent you another 
letter in which it withdrew its earlier response. 
s action wa 


Mr. John F. Henning 
P. 3 
February I, 1978 
based on the fact that on November 7, 1975, a Los Angeles County 
Superior Court judge had issued a preliminary injunction prohibit-
ing the Commission from interpreting the concept of arranging for 
a contribution to include the mere act of reco~~ending that a 
contribution be made. 
Institute of Governmental Advocates v. 
Younger, No. C 110,052 (November 7, 1975). 
In its letter of 
December 3, 1975, the Commission also agreed to seek a prorrpt reso-
lution of the issues raised in the Younger case relative to the 
meaning of the term "arrange" as used in Gov. Code Section 86202, 
to refrain from disapproving the proposed amendments to the Fed-
eration's Constitution prior to the resolution of these issues 
>and to take no enforcement action inconsistent with the prelimi-
nary injunction issued in Younger. 
On June 21, 1977, the Californ 
Court of Appeal af-
firmed the Superior Court's judgment in the Younger case, albeit 
on different grounds. 
Institute of Governmental Advocates v. 
Younger, 70 Cal. App. 3d 878 (1977). 
The Court of Appeal con-
eluded that the C,~mmission had correctly interpretGd the term 
"arrange," as used in Gov. Code Section 86202, to include recom-
mending that a contribution be made, but that so interpreted 
the Section created impermissible "chilling effects" on the 
right of free speech and, therefore, was unconstitutional. 
70 Cal. App. 3d at 884. 
The Commission has decided not to ap-
peal this decision and, thus, it is final. 
Having reviewed the proposed amendmen~s to the Federa-
tion's Constitution in light of the ruling in Younger, the Com-
mission has concluded that the procedures established by those 
a~endments will not result in violations of the Political Reform 
Act. 
The a..'nendments permit lobbyists to have "voice but no vote" 
during the 
Federation's ; recommendation and endorsement process 
and to provide "advice and information" during the process by 
which the Committee on Political Education ("COPE") and t 
three-
member Subcommittee on State and Local Office Political Contribu-
tions (the IISubcommittee") determine what amount of funds to de-
vote to political contributions and which candidates shall be the 
recipients of those funds. 
Additionally, the amendments provide 
that the Director of COPE shall be an ex-officio member of the Sub-
committee with "voice but no vote," 
Although these procedures may 
enable the Federation's lobbyists to "arrange" contributions, 
actions which would constitute the arranging are permissible under 
the court's ruling in Younger and, therefore, would not en~ender 
any enforcement actions by the Co~~ission. 
We do want to caution you, however, that the ruling in 
the Younger case is re 
ively narrow in scope and that 
pro-
hibition against arranging a contribution t although not appl 
Ie 
, 


Mr. John F. Henning 
P. 
February 1, 1978 
to certain types of conduct, still is viable. 
Thus, by way of 
example, a lobbyist still is prohibited from discussing a contri-
bution with a potential recipient and then recou~ending to his 
employer that a contribution be made. 
Also, a lobbyist can~ot 
sign or deliver the check containing a contribution. 
An additional comment may be pertinent with respect to 
the Federation. 
The procedures pursuant to which the Federation 
makes campaign contributions inevitably involve the Secretary-
Treasurer of the Federation in the decision making process con-
,cerning such contributions. 
When the Secretary-Treasurer a 
0 
is a lobbyist, care should be exercised bye'Jeryone involved in 
the Federation's campaign contribution decisions to IL~it the 
Secretary-Treasurer's involvement to providing advice, informa-
tion and recommendations. 
The court's decision in Younger makes it unnecessary 
for the Co~~ission to issue a formal opinion in response to your 
original request. 
Accordingly, this letter, which has been re-
viewed and approved by the members of the Commission, shall repre-
sent the Cormnission' s response. 
If rem have any further questions, 
please do not hesitate to contact me.

sections.question: Whether certain proposed amendments to the California Labor Federation's Constitution altering the process by which the Federation would make political contributions would satisfy the requirements of the Political Reform Act. The proposed amendments would: (1) provide that Officers, Executive Council members, and Convention delegates who are "lobbyists" shall have voice but no vote in the political recommendation and endorsement process; and (2) establish procedures whereby the Standing Committee on Political Education would determine contribution limits and select a three-member Subcommittee to make contributions, with voting restricted to non-lobbyist members, and the Director prohibited from making recommendations as to amounts.
sections.conclusion: The Commission has concluded that the procedures established by the proposed amendments will not result in violations of the Political Reform Act. The amendments permit lobbyists to have "voice but no vote" during the Federation's recommendation and endorsement process and to provide "advice and information" during the process by which the Committee on Political Education and the three-member Subcommittee determine what amount of funds to devote to political contributions and which candidates shall be recipients of those funds. Although these procedures may enable the Federation's lobbyists to "arrange" contributions, actions which would constitute arranging are permissible under the court's ruling in the Younger case and therefore would not engender enforcement actions by the Commission. However, the Commission cautioned that the Younger ruling is relatively narrow in scope and that certain prohibited conduct remains, such as a lobbyist discussing a contribution with a potential recipient and then recommending to his employer that a contribution be made, or signing or delivering contribution checks.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether proposed amendments to the California Labor Federation's Constitution that restrict lobbyist voting rights in political contribution decisions while allowing them voice and advisory input comply with the Political Reform Act, particularly regarding the prohibition on "arranging" contributions.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The proposed amendments comply with the Political Reform Act under the Younger case ruling, which held that recommending contributions does not violate the statute. However, the FPPC cautioned that certain conduct remains prohibited, including direct discussions with potential recipients followed by recommendations, and handling of contribution checks.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is a formal FPPC advice letter with clear question and conclusion sections. Some OCR artifacts present but do not significantly impact extraction. Letter discusses withdrawal of prior response and judicial ruling that informed final opinion. Contains multiple citations to FPPC opinions and case law.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether certain proposed amendments to the California Labor Federation's Constitution altering the process by which the Federation would make political contributions would satisfy the requirements of the Political Reform Act. The proposed amendments would: (1) provide that Officers, Executive Council members, and Convention delegates who are "lobbyists" shall have voice but no vote in the political recommendation and endorsement process; and (2) establish procedures whereby the Standing Committee on Political Education would determine contribution limits and select a three-member Subcommittee to make contributions, with voting restricted to non-lobbyist members, and the Director prohibited from making recommendations as to amounts.

CONCLUSION: The Commission has concluded that the procedures established by the proposed amendments will not result in violations of the Political Reform Act. The amendments permit lobbyists to have "voice but no vote" during the Federation's recommendation and endorsement process and to provide "advice and information" during the process by which the Committee on Political Education and the three-member Subcommittee determine what amount of funds to devote to political contributions and which candidates shall be recipients of those funds. Although these procedures may enable the Federation's lobbyists to "arrange" contributions, actions which would constitute arranging are permissible under the court's ruling in the Younger case and therefore would not engender enforcement actions by the Commission. However, the Commission cautioned that the Younger ruling is relatively narrow in scope and that certain prohibited conduct remains, such as a lobbyist discussing a contribution with a potential recipient and then recommending to his employer that a contribution be made, or signing or delivering contribution checks.

embedding.summary: This is an FPPC advice letter responding to a 1975 opinion request by the California Labor Federation regarding proposed constitutional amendments to govern political contribution procedures. The Commission ultimately approved the amendments after a 1977 court ruling in Institute of Governmental Advocates v. Younger narrowed the prohibition on "arranging" contributions to exclude recommendations.
